stories.title: The Glassblower's Reckoning
stories.text:
In the Lampwick Quarter of Keshtir, where furnace-heat has warped the cobblestones for three generations, Mella strikes the kiln with her hammer. The glass-vessel inside shatters—not by accident. The Dominion auditor watches from the doorway, ledger open. "You've destroyed a consciousness-fragment vessel. Sedition tax, doubled." His fingers drum the page. Mella turns, sweat-bright, copper dust on her knuckles. "The glass was flawed. It sang wrong. If I'd sealed a fragment inside, it would have corrupted. You want corrupted consciousness-currency, auditor? That's the real theft."

A lie, wrapped in craft-logic he cannot dispute. But the auditor's eyes narrow. Mella has destroyed seventeen vessels this month—each time a different excuse, each time the Dominion's inventory shrinks. He leaves. She waits until his footsteps fade, then kneels beside the broken glass. Inside the shards lies a fragment—not corrupted, but *hidden*. She wraps it in cloth that remembers pre-Ascension silk and carries it through the Quarter's winding streets. In the old Well-House beneath the Lampwick, bone-singers wait. Moriel takes the fragment and nods.

stories.title: The Reef-Keeper's Gambit
stories.text:
Captain Merenna floats in the pearl-chamber of the Sapphire Deep, her echo-selves arranged in concentric circles around her. Three reflections of one command. One accord, fragmenting. "The Tear weakens," Echo-Merenna says. "Sacrifice the surface-dwellers. One bloodletting, and we seal the boundary for another thousand years."

"No," says Merenna-Mirror. "Demand yearly tribute. Make them *choose* to feed the bargain. That is mercy—and obedience."

Merenna herself says nothing. She holds a pearl—the last one carved with the old covenant-mark, before the Remaking fractured even the deep-dwellers' consensus. Around her, young deepkin wait. They do not yet know which version of their captain will lead them. She rises from the pearl-bed, her gills fluttering, and swims toward the Tear itself—that wound in reality holding back the abyss. It *sings* now, discordant. Not a seal. A dying thing. Merenna presses her palm against it. The boundary-water burns. She understands, at last. The old covenant was never meant to last forever. It was always a temporary bridge, bought with sacrifice because sacrifice was the only language both worlds spoke.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Winter Gift
stories.text:
Sorrath lies coiled in her Ironspine lair, watching the last of her hoard catch fire. Not burning—*transforming*. The gold that once filled three caverns now shifts, liquefies, becomes something else: consciousness-fragments sealed in bottles of starlight-glass, each one a refugee's scattered self. She weeps. Wyverns do not weep. For three hundred years, Sorrath accumulated. Every coin, every jewel, every golden artifact—proof of dominion, of worth, of permanence. The Remaking took all of it in an instant and revealed the truth: her hoard was a prison, and she the jailer of her own isolation. A child enters the cavern—a refugee girl no more than seven winters, drawn by heat and hunger. Sorrath does not reach out to grab. She moves aside, letting the girl see the transformed treasures. "Take what you need."

The girl's eyes widen. She reaches for a bottle containing a fragment that *glows* with recognition—a memory of her mother's face. "My mother," the girl whispers. "A part of her," Sorrath says, her voice like stone-grinding. "Others carry other parts.

stories.title: The Locksmith's Inheritance
stories.text: Thessa takes the bone-picks from her father's workbench and feels the weight of forty years of theft in her hands. Gorven the Conjurer was legendary—locks that had held tyrants' gold fell open at his touch. Now he is scattered, a consciousness-fragment split between two ledgers, alive in neither. But his picks remember. She has one night to open the Dominion's vault beneath Keshtir's administrative heart. One chance to steal what the auditors stole from her: proof that her father existed before the Remaking fractured him. A ledger-entry. A name written in a way that does not contradict another world's version. The vault door is bone-resonant. Seven layers of lock, each carved with Elder Tongue syllables. Each one a puzzle that *changes* if you try it wrong twice. Thessa approaches the first lock as her father taught her: listen to the metal. Feel where the pins want to fall. Whisper to the mechanism, not force it. The bone-picks warm in her hands. She works through the night, and by dawn she has reached the seventh lock. Her fingers are bleeding.

stories.title: The Shepherd's Mercy
stories.text:
Kestran tends his flocks in the High Meadows of the Steppe Marches, where the wind speaks in three languages and the snow never fully melts. He is old—older than the Remaking, older than most kingdoms. The sheep know his voice. The grass remembers his footsteps. One dawn, a creature stumbles out of the snow: not quite wolf, not quite deer, something the Remaking *made*. Its eyes are wrong—too many facets, like broken glass. It staggers toward his flock. Kestran draws his bow—the one inherited from his grandmother, carved from wood that remembers battlefields. It chooses true shots. But as he nocks the arrow, he remembers something his grandmother told him: "A weapon that never misses is only merciful if you *choose* what mercy means."

He lowers the bow. The creature collapses before the sheep, too weak to hunt. Kestran carries it to his shelter and feeds it. For three days it eats, and on the fourth it speaks—not in words, but in frequency-song, the language the Remaking left behind. It is a deepkin, transformed.

stories.title: The Glassblower's Daughter
stories.text: Missa worked in the Lampwick Quarter's furnace-house, where kilns sang with frequencies that made consciousness-fragments glow inside their vessels. The Dominion auditors had requisitioned her mother's last batch—three hundred vials of memory-glass meant for refugee shelter, now sealed in state vaults beneath Keshtir. She was no thief. The glass didn't break. Instead it sang—a frequency that matched the Elder Tongue syllables her grandmother had whispered into every kiln-stone. The auditors heard it too. The real fragments she'd hidden in plain sight—woven into bolts of cloth that looked like ordinary linen, stacked among the potter's ware in the market quarter. That night, refugees began arriving at the furnace-house. Not to beg. To work. They carried fragments of their own shattered selves and asked Missa to help them make vessels that wouldn't sing to auditors. She taught them the old songs—the ones her grandmother had carved into bone. By dawn, the furnace-house had become a counter-workshop. The Dominion would learn of it eventually.

stories.title: The Reef-Singer's Mercy
stories.text: Captain Merenna of the Sapphire Deep had negotiated a thousand treaties, but none like this. The Tear of the Drowned God was failing—the boundary between abyss and surface cracking with each tide. The Deepkin Council demanded sacrifice: a willing volunteer to renew the seal with blood. Instead, Merenna proposed something radical: a living covenant. Each dawn, a deepkin and a surface-dweller would meet at the boundary-waters, would speak a vow of choosing each other anew, would strengthen the seal not through death but through daily decision. The surface kingdoms laughed. The deepkin traditionalists wept with fury. But Merenna had already chosen her partner—a human cartographer named Aldrin, who had deliberately falsified maps to prevent the Ninth Iteration's completion. He'd blinded himself rather than chart prophecy. She'd decided that blindness and refusal were the truest forms of sight. On the first dawn, they met at the living coral platform where Merenna's reflection-self had once drowned. Aldrin could not see the bioluminescent gardens below, but he could hear them—ancient frequencies singing older than kingdoms.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Gold-Less Hoard
stories.text: Skarath had spent three centuries accumulating. Gold, pearls, star-glass, consciousness-fragments in crystal vessels—mountains of wealth stacked in the Ironspine caverns until she could barely move among her own hoard. Gold became ash. And Skarath discovered something worse than loss: she discovered that without the hoard's weight, she was hollow. She began collecting other things. Stories. A refugee told her about a village burned for refusing a warlord's levy; Skarath kept that account in her memory, refused to forget it. A bone-singer brought word of seven scattered families searching for their origins; Skarath offered her caverns as sanctuary. A merchant arrived with news that a tyrannical prince had been overthrown; Skarath wept—actual tears that fell on the worthless gold like rain. Over weeks, the cavern transformed. The ash-gold remained, but Skarath surrounded it with shelter: carved stone platforms for refugees, heated pools that eased the wasting-sickness, stone tablets inscribed with the names of the dead so no one would be forgotten again. Instead of fleeing, Skarath invited him inside. "Count what you find," she said.

stories.title: The Deepkin Breach
stories.text: The Tear of the Drowned God was singing wrong. Instead of a steady harmonic, it shrieked—the sound of something ancient and wounded. Captain Kalith of the deepkin guard arrived at the Council of Depths with grim news: the boundary was not merely weakening. Something was actively trying to breach it from below. The conservatives demanded immediate sacrifice. Kill a surface-dweller volunteer, spill blood on the seal, renew the pact through death. But Kalith had a different idea. The breach wasn't random. It was orchestrated. She descended alone to the deepest vault where the Tear hung like a pearl suspended in impossible geometry. And there, clawing at the barrier from the abyss-side, she found them: the pre-human dead. They were singing in frequencies older than the deepkin language itself. They wanted out. They wanted to remember. They wanted to teach again. Kalith realized the truth that terrified the Council: the Tear wasn't failing because of neglect.

stories.title: The Widow's Compass & the Nomad's Reckoning
stories.text:
The widow had hidden herself in the high moors for three years, tending gardens that shouldn't exist in such cold. Sorenna carried two compasses now: her grandmother's bone-and-silver original, and one made of starlight-glass that pointed to the pre-human boundary her dead husband had died defending. The nomad found her because he was dying. His name was Yareth, and he'd spent a decade riding the Steppe Marches as a hired blade—until the Remaking made gold worthless and mercenary work a joke. He'd heard rumor of a woman who grew crops using pre-Ascension magic, and he wagered his last coin that she'd shelter him. Sorenna didn't turn him away. But she didn't shelter him either. Instead, she handed him the starlight-glass compass and walked him to the ravine where her husband had fallen defending the boundary. "You want redemption," she said. "Not mercy."

Yareth understood. The boundary between human and pre-human worlds was fracturing. The forest-keepers couldn't hold it alone. The bone-singers were spread too thin.
